Behold the  "Smellicopter": A wee drone that's been outfitted with a live antenna from a moth, which lets it navigate towards smells.
 It worked — and when they compared its performance to an artificial, human-made smell sensor, the moth antenna won out: The drone navigated more quickly towards an odor.
 Why try to improve upon nature using biomimickry, when you can use nature itself? Judged on the arid plains of engineering innovation, this is clever as heck.
 Judged using a more Freudian standard, this experiment fairly trembles with uncanniness. There's something  so very unsettling about seeing a cyborg robot outfitted with live moth parts. Apparently the moth antenna remains active for four hours after it's been removed from the insect (they anesthetize the moths, BTW). To get a signal out of it they just run an electrical current though it. In a way, it's this latter detail that I find most morbidly fascinating: That parts of our biology work perfectly fine as severable, discrete plug-in boards for electronics, even adapting nicely to the voltage specs of microprocessors.
 I don't want to harsh on this experiment too much — it's genuinely interesting. And as they point out, improving olfactory sensing has some very practical uses, including sniffing out the unexploded ordinances that ruin people's lives in former combat zones.
  "Nature really blows our human-made odor sensors out of the water," said lead author Melanie Anderson, a UW doctoral student in mechanical engineering. "By using an actual moth antenna with Smellicopter, we're able to get the best of both worlds: the sensitivity of a biological organism on a robotic platform where we can control its motion."
 The moth uses its antennae to sense chemicals in its environment and navigate toward sources of food or potential mates.
 "Cells in a moth antenna amplify chemical signals," said co-author Thomas Daniel, a UW professor of biology who co-supervises Anderson's doctoral research. "The moths do it really efficiently — one scent molecule can trigger lots of cellular responses, and that's the trick. This process is super efficient, specific and fast." [snip]
 "From a robotics perspective, this is genius," said co-author and co-advisor Sawyer Fuller, a UW assistant professor of mechanical engineering. "The classic approach in robotics is to add more sensors, and maybe build a fancy algorithm or use machine learning to estimate wind direction. It turns out, all you need is to add a fin."

note_en:
Get ready for   more Star Wars adventures with Ahsoka Tano. The popular character -- from   The Mandalorian,   Star Wars: The Clone Wars and   Star Wars: Rebels -- is finally getting her own series,  Star Wars: Ahsoka, starring   Rosario Dawson. It's set within the timeline for The Mandalorian and will debut on  Disney Plus around Christmas 2021.
 Disney revealed the news Thursday during an  investor presentation, where it also announced plans for upcoming movie releases -- both theatrical and streaming on Disney Plus.
 Ahsoka, a new Original Series, starring  @RosarioDawson and set within the timeline of  @TheMandalorian, is coming to  @DisneyPlus.  pic.twitter.com/jD8byvNvDP
— Star Wars (@starwars)  December 10, 2020
 Ahsoka made her live-action debut in   The Mandalorian episode titled The Jedi. In the episode, Mando ( Pedro Pascal) continues  his quest to bring   Baby Yoda (aka The Child) to former Jedi  Ahsoka Tano in the years following  Return of the Jedi.
 Ahsoka communicates with Baby Yoda through the Force, and we learn his real name: Grogu. Ahsoka reveals that Grogu was raised in the Jedi Temple on Coruscant, and was trained by many masters. Grogu was hidden when the Empire Order 66 killed off most of the Jedi, as depicted in the 2005 Star Wars movie  Revenge of the Sith.
 Entertain your brain with the coolest news from streaming to superheroes, memes to video games.
 Dawson's portrayal of the animated character meant some   changes needed to be made to Ahsoka's appearance for the transition to a live-action format so stunt work during lightsaber fights could be performed more easily.
 Not much has been revealed as of yet about the new Ahsoka Tano live-action series for Disney Plus, but fingers crossed we get to see more characters from both Star Wars: The Clone Wars and Star Wars: Rebels pop up in the new adventures.
 Disney said Disney Plus will have   10 Star Wars series "over the next few years."
 This is a developing story; check back for updates. CNET's full coverage includes    all the news from Disney's event .

10年前，Facebook高管接触了使用Facebook数据的流行应用程序的创建者Ali Partovi，其最后通threat极具威胁。根据法律文件，将您的公司出售给我们，否则我们将关闭您。 Partovi的应用程序iLike已为“顶”按钮建立了前身。
  Partovi拒绝了这个提议。不久之后，Facebook终止了iLike所依赖的功能，促使Partovi将其初创公司出售给Myspace，价格仅为其先前价值的一小部分。然后，Facebook建立了自己的“喜欢”按钮，模仿iLike。
 谈判（当Facebook只是一个小得多的参与者时）是消除竞争的强硬策略的早期证据，该竞争使社交网络发展到今天：一个平台，该平台将全世界三分之一以上的人口视为其家庭的每月用户的应用程序，其中包括WhatsApp消息传递和照片共享服务Instagram。
   现在，这种行为成为48家州检察长和联邦贸易委员会提起的广泛反托拉斯诉讼的主题。诉讼称，该公司是一家垄断企业，它通过多年的反竞争行为和非法收购滥用了其市场支配力，使Facebook成为世界上最大的社交网络，同时剥夺了其他用户的选择。
  这些诉讼特别要求分拆Facebook分别在2014年和2012年购买的WhatsApp和Instagram。它引用了Facebook首席执行官马克·扎克伯格（Mark Zuckerberg）的话，认为两家公司都是需要消除的日益严重的威胁。
  Facebook周三表示，FTC的行动将对创新产生寒蝉效应，收购案当时已被包括FTC在内的监管机构审查。该公司在一份声明中说：“现在，很多年后，似乎没有考虑已解决的法律或对创新和投资的后果，该机构说这是错的，并希望解决。”
   扎克伯格在发给该公司网站的员工电子邮件中说，谷歌，谷歌，Twitter，Snapchat，苹果iMessage，TikTok和YouTube都面临着令人难以置信的竞争格局。他还说，他预计诉讼不会对人们的工作造成任何影响。 
社交网络已经习惯了购买，威胁，暗中监视和直接复制竞争对手，这种策略是如此成功，以至于一些投资者在最近几年说，再也没有必要筹集资金或构建社交应用程序了。
  风险投资公司New Enterprise Associates的执行合伙人斯科特·桑德尔（Scott Sandell）告诉微软，Facebook会像微软在遇到监管机构时一样，对竞争威胁进行“猛烈抨击”，他一直担任微软Windows 95的产品经理。在2017年的采访中发布。
   尽管扎克伯格在收购公司时向WhatsApp和Instagram的创始人承诺独立，但如今这些应用程序已完全合并到Facebook中，这在技术上构成了挑战。
 Facebook评论家和前投资者罗杰·麦克纳姆（Roger McNamee）说：“ Facebook即将发现破坏美国民主和公共健康的后果。” “他们争先恐后地通过合并关键产品的后端使监管机构的工作更加困难。”
 在公司的大部分历史中，扎克伯格一直专注于扩大公司的用户群，该公司在公司成立八年后，在2012年公开募股时已飙升至50亿用户，这是当时闻所未闻的轨迹。对于一家技术公司。但是Facebook受到Path和Instagram等新兴公司的威胁。
  根据FTC的诉讼，扎克伯格对Instagram专注于视觉交流印象深刻，告诉一位代理人，该公司“正在从如何帮助人们拍摄精美照片的角度解决这一问题”，这与Facebook的“花哨的”方法不同。 。 Facebook并未征服智能手机。
   扎克伯格认为，使用手机的摄像头是社交网络中的一种日益增长的趋势，该公司不希望落后。根据这些文件，他写道：“一个令人担忧的趋势是每天都有大量的人在使用Instagram。”扎克伯格说，落后的前景“确实令人恐惧”。 
扎克伯格以10亿美元的价格收购Instagram，这使它的16名员工大吃一惊，并带有独立的承诺。
 但是在公司被收购后不久，来自Facebook的团队加入进来，并开始引入照片标记和其他Facebook功能。知情人士说，Instagram的产品团队认为这些功能也被Facebook识别。诉讼文件显示，Facebook还阻止Instagram引入与Facebook竞争的功能。
   Instagram的独立性冲突最终导致其创始人Kevin Systrom和Mike Krieger于2018年辞职。
 多年来，这两个应用程序已完全集成在一起，可以共享用户数据，广告系统和合并消息传递服务。
  2013年，Facebook通过购买一个鲜为人知的应用程序Onavo加快了其增长战略。 Onavo通过提供安全的虚拟专用网络服务向公众推销自己。
 但是，在后端，它还跟踪用户的在线活动，包括他们使用了哪些应用程序，从而可以查看哪些应用程序变得流行。根据诉讼，高管们将收到有关应用程序迅速普及的“早起的鸟儿”报告，并迅速了解到WhatsApp每天吸引100万用户，其中许多在发展中国家。 （经过公众审查后，Facebook在2019年关闭了Onavo。）
   Facebook的高管们担心会受到具有社交网络特征的移动消息服务的干扰。 FTC文件显示，一位高管将移动消息服务的兴起描述为“对我的产品构成的最大威胁，这是我在Facebook呆了5年的经历……我们都感到恐惧”。 
另一位Facebook高管称WhatsApp首席执行官Jan Koum是该公司最大的竞争对手，而Facebook最大的问题将在于WhatsApp是否落入Google手中。
 当Facebook在2014年以190亿美元的价格收购WhatsApp时，这是硅谷历史上规模最大的收购之一。
  扎克伯格告诉WhatsApp联合创始人布莱恩·阿克顿（Brian Acton），他不会将Facebook的数据与WhatsApp的数据合并，这是阿克顿向正在审查该交易的欧洲监管机构重申的承诺。
  但是几年之后，这正是Facebook所做的。这一举动以及其他损害WhatsApp独立性的行动，导致Acton和Koum退出了公司和Facebook，并于2017年被欧盟罚款1.22亿美元。
 随着Facebook通过收购而增长，它还切断了依赖其平台的较小竞争对手。长期以来，Facebook一直为开发人员提供访问其平台（称为API）的权限，但通常对开发人员如何使用数据并不满意。美国联邦贸易委员会的诉讼声称，Facebook利用其影响力来发挥其力量并损害竞争。
  美国联邦贸易委员会（FTC）的指控呼应了应用程序开发商SixFourThree提起的先前案件，该指控称该公司被突然更改以限制其数据时被Facebook杀死。
  2011年，Facebook更改了政策，以禁止可能与Facebook竞争的应用程序使用其数据。政策变化是如此突然，以至于一位员工提出抗议：“我认为……向世界（可能更重要的是向我们的员工）传达了一个信息，我们害怕我们无法凭借自己的优势进行竞争，”诉讼 
但据FTC出具的文件，Facebook知道它可以做自己想做的事情，因为到那时，它的API对于任何有抱负的社交应用来说都变得至关重要。  尽管最近几年Facebook出现了问题，但硅谷却鲜有人愿意公开批评该公司的商业行为。  但是影响已在法庭记录中浮出水面。 拒绝发表评论的Partovi在对SixFourThree诉讼的证词中表示，他从未忘记Facebook的恐吓手段。 他说：“受到威胁时，只需要一次。” “所以从那时起，我们就生活在那种威胁之下。”
note_en:
Facebook executives approached Ali Partovi, the creator of a popular app that used Facebook’s data, a decade ago with a threatening ultimatum. Sell your company to us or we will shut you down, according to legal filings. Partovi’s app, iLike, had built a predecessor to the “like” button.
  Partovi refused the offer. Shortly after, Facebook discontinued features that iLike relied upon, pushing Partovi to sell his start-up to Myspace for a fraction of its previous value. Facebook then built its own “like” button, modeled after iLike.
 The negotiations — when Facebook was a much smaller player — are early evidence of the hardball tactics to neutralize competition that got the social network to where it is today: a platform that counts more than a third of the world’s population as monthly users of its family of apps, which include WhatsApp messaging and the photo-sharing service Instagram.
   That behavior is now the subject of  sweeping antitrust lawsuits filed Wednesday from 48 state attorneys general and the Federal Trade Commission. The suits allege that the company is a monopoly that abused its market power through years of anti-competitive conduct and illegal acquisitions, enabling Facebook to become the world’s largest social network while stripping users of alternatives.
  The suits call specifically for breaking up WhatsApp and Instagram, which Facebook bought in 2014 and 2012, respectively. It quotes Facebook chief executive Mark Zuckerberg identifying both companies as growing threats that needed to be neutralized.
  Facebook on Wednesday said the FTC’s actions would have a chilling effect on innovation and that the acquisitions had been reviewed by regulators at the time, including the FTC. “Now, many years later, with seemingly no regard for settled law or the consequences to innovation and investment, the agency is saying it got it wrong and wants a do-over,” the company said in a statement.
   In an email to staff that was also posted on the company’s website, Zuckerberg said that the company faced an incredibly competitive landscape, noting Google, Twitter, Snapchat, Apple’s iMessage, TikTok, and YouTube. He also said that he didn’t anticipate any impact from the lawsuit on people’s jobs.
 The social network has made a habit of buying up, threatening, spying on and outright copying rivals, a strategy so successful that  some investors have said in recent years that there was no point in even funding or building social apps anymore.
  Facebook would “pounce”  on competitive threats in the same way Microsoft did when it got into trouble with regulators, Scott Sandell, managing partner of the venture capital firm New Enterprise Associates, who was product manager for Microsoft’s Windows 95 until 1995,  told The Washington Post in a 2017 interview.
   Although Zuckerberg made promises of independence to the founders of WhatsApp and Instagram when he purchased the companies, today those apps are fully merged into Facebook, making a breakup technically challenging.
 “Facebook is about to find out if there will be consequences to undermining American democracy and public health,” said Facebook critic and former investor Roger McNamee. “They have scrambled to make the job of regulators more difficult by merging the back end of key products.”
 For most of the company’s history, Zuckerberg was laser-focused on growing the company’s user base, which had skyrocketed to a half-billion users at the time of its 2012 public offering, eight years after its founding — a trajectory that was then unheard of for a technology company. But Facebook was threatened by rising upstarts, including Path and Instagram.
  Zuckerberg was impressed by Instagram’s exclusive focus on visual communication, telling a deputy that the company was “approaching this problem from the perspective of how to help people take beautiful photos,” which was different from Facebook’s “gimmicky” approach, according to the FTC suit. Facebook had not conquered smartphones.
   Zuckerberg thought that utilizing the phone’s camera was a growing trend in social networking, one the company did not want to fall behind on. “One concerning trend is that a huge number of people are using Instagram every day,” he wrote, according to the documents. The prospect of falling behind was “really scary,” Zuckerberg said.
 Zuckerberg’s purchase of Instagram for $1 billion, which took its 16 employees by surprise, came with promises of independence.
 But shortly after the company was acquired, a team from Facebook came in and began  introducing photo-tagging and other Facebook features. Instagram’s product teams thought these features were too identified with Facebook, according to people familiar with the meetings. Facebook also prevented Instagram from introducing features that would compete with Facebook, according to documents in the lawsuit.
   The clash over Instagram’s independence eventually led its founders, Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger, to quit in 2018.
 Over the years, the two apps have become fully integrated, sharing user data, ad systems and merging messaging services.
  In 2013, Facebook ramped up its growth strategy by purchasing a little-known app called Onavo. Onavo marketed itself to the public as providing secure virtual private networking services.
 On the back end, however, it also tracked users’ online activity, including what apps they used, allowing it to see which ones were becoming popular. Executives would receive “Early Bird” reports on apps surging in popularity, according to the lawsuit, and quickly learned that WhatsApp was gaining 1 million users per day, many in the developing world. (Facebook shut down Onavo in 2019 after public scrutiny.)
   Facebook executives feared being usurped by mobile messaging services, which were taking on the characteristics of social networks. One executive described the rise of mobile messaging services as “the biggest threat to our product that I’ve ever seen in my 5 years here at Facebook … we’re all terrified,” according to the FTC documents.
 Another Facebook executive described WhatsApp chief executive Jan Koum as the company’s biggest competitor, and that Facebook’s greatest problem would be if WhatsApp landed in the hands of Google.
 When Facebook acquired WhatsApp in 2014, for $19 billion, it was one of the largest acquisitions in the history of Silicon Valley.
  Zuckerberg told WhatsApp co-founder Brian Acton that he would not merge data from Facebook with data from WhatsApp, a promise that Acton reiterated to European regulators, who were scrutinizing the deal.
  But a few years later, that is exactly what Facebook did. That move — and other actions to compromise WhatsApp’s independence — led Acton and Koum to  quit the company and Facebook to be fined $122 million by the European Union in 2017.
 As Facebook grew through acquisitions, it also cut off smaller competitors that depended on its platform. Facebook had long given developers access to its platform, known as an API, but was often fickle with how developers could use the data. The FTC’s lawsuit alleges that Facebook used its clout to leverage its power and hurt competition.
  The FTC’s charges echo claims from a previous case brought by an app developer, SixFourThree, which charged that it was killed off by Facebook when the company made a sudden change to restrict its data.
  In 2011, Facebook changed its policies so that apps which could potentially compete with Facebook were no longer allowed to use its data. The policy change was so abrupt that one employee protested, “I think it … sends a message to the world (and probably more importantly to our employees) that we’re scared that we can’t compete on our own merits,” according to the lawsuit
  But Facebook knew it could do what it wanted because by that time its API had become essential for any aspiring social app, according to documents unearthed by the FTC.
 Even as Facebook’s problems have mounted in recent years, few in Silicon Valley have been willing to publicly criticize the company’s business practices.
 But the impact has surfaced in court records. In a deposition in the SixFourThree lawsuit, Partovi, who declined to comment, said he never forgot Facebook’s intimidation tactics. “When you’re threatened, it only takes once,” he said. “So from that point on, we lived under that threat.”
title: 华盛顿邮报

note:
多年来，批评人士一直认为，Facebook对Instagram的收购是控制公司的错失良机。但是现在，他们将有机会实际撤消该操作。周三下午，联邦贸易委员会与48位州检察长一起，要求法院命令剥离Instagram和WhatsApp，这实际上是在迫使Facebook撤回这两项收购，然后将其转回独立公司。
 监管机构距离实现这一目标还有很长的路要走，但第一次，Facebook就有可能不容忽视。对于Instagram用户，这引发了一个既令人震惊又令人着迷的问题：如果没有Facebook，Instagram会是什么样？
 自2012年被收购以来的几年中，Instagram从与Facebook的关系中受益匪浅，获得了巨大的规模，并摆脱了实现盈利的压力。即使到现在，Facebook的支持对于Instagram积极的产品开发周期和低调的内容审核挑战也至关重要。如果监管机构获得成功，Instagram将不得不从头开始建立更多的基础。
 同时，有理由认为，没有Facebook的负担，Instagram可能会变得更好，并且可能会在Menlo Park泡沫之外蓬勃发展。 Facebook的名称和领带意味着平台混乱无序和品牌梦night。由于母公司的行为，Instagram每天都留在Facebook家族中，这又面临着用户强烈反对的风险。没有这种头痛，Instagram可以一开始就专注于使它成为流行应用程序的原因。
  案件宣布后不到24小时，Facebook还没有对反托拉斯诉讼提出充分的辩护。但该公司的总法律顾问在昨天的一篇博客文章中提供了对该案的早期预览，认为分拆既不公平也不可行。总法律顾问詹妮弗·纽斯特德（Jennifer Newstead）写道：“没有一个美国反托拉斯执行者以前有过这样的案子，这是有充分理由的。 “联邦贸易委员会和各州一直待命多年，而Facebook投资了数十亿美元和数百万小时，将Instagram和WhatsApp制作成用户今天喜欢的应用程序。”
 但是，剥离一家大企业并不像Newstead听起来那么困难。无论是在2017年惠普分拆其企业业务，还是IAC在今年早些时候分拆IAC的Match Group，公司都会在看到利润的时候一直这样做。当然，根据政府的命令，它有所不同，但即使在80年代贝尔解体中也有一个较新的先例。
 经过八年的公司整合，分拆Instagram肯定会很困难。 Facebook在2012年四分之一的时间内以10亿美元的价格收购了Instagram，从那以后，Facebook一直试图将这两个应用紧密集成。从一开始，它就通过标记从Instagram发布到Facebook的带有该应用程序链接的照片来提高Instagram的知名度，并提示人们在Instagram上关注他们的Facebook朋友。 
莎拉·弗里尔（Sarah Frier）在她的书《无过滤器：Instagram的内幕》中写道：“ Instagram的购买比构建起来更容易，因为一旦网络建立起来，就没有理由加入较小的网络了。” “它成为社会基础设施的一部分。”
  Facebook还对该公司进行了财务支持，并分享了其广告技术来帮助其实际赚钱，而这并不是Instagram在收购前所做的。但是，尽管Facebook最终终止了促销支持，但其网络效应已经占据了上风：Instagram现在拥有10亿用户和遍布各地的广告。在最重要的领域，Facebook的工作已经完成。如果Instagram现在分崩离析，这些关键的变化将保持不变。
 与Facebook的其余集成似乎对Facebook本身有利，而不是Instagram。近年来，Facebook更加努力地链接这两个应用程序，从而允许用户在Facebook和Instagram之间交叉发布其内容。现在可以通过Instagram使用Facebook Messenger，将平台捆绑在一个统一的消息系统中。但是对于Instagram用户而言，这些集成主要是使Facebook变得不可避免。
 正是这些紧密的联系促使Instagram联合创始人凯文·西斯特罗姆（Kevin Systrom）和迈克·克里格（Mike Krieger）辞职。据报道，在Systrom休陪产假期间，Facebook取消了对增长的支持并测试了新的设计和功能，两位高管均辞职，包括汉堡包菜单和位置跟踪。
 Messenger集成是用户可能处于不利地位的最明显的地方-Facebook上的人无法向Instagram上的朋友发送消息，反之亦然-但是该功能是相当新的功能，只有用户可以间歇性地看到。在此之前，用户似乎对分别关闭各自平台的朋友感到满意，因此很难说将这些孤岛放回原处会很痛苦。
  最大的问题是，如果没有Facebook的资金，Instagram是否可以生存。 Facebook从未公布过Instagram的财务状况，因此我们不知道该平台是否可以自己获利。然而，彭博社今年初写道，Instagram在2019年创造了Facebook收入的四分之一以上，或约200亿美元的广告收入。这显然很重要，而且比Snap等大多数社交竞争对手自己带来的收入还多。我们不知道Instagram的独立运营成本，但是仅凭200亿美元的广告收入，不难想象该团队在开发新产品时仍可以设法获利。
 如果没有Facebook，Instagram可能会挣扎的一个领域是其蓬勃发展的商业业务。这是Facebook基础设施肯定可以帮助Instagram以及该公司必须进行投资的领域。目前，Facebook Pay允许Instagram用户直接从平台购买产品。 Facebook尚未向第三方应用程序许可Facebook Pay支付，因此Instagram必须要么继续与PayPal建立关系，PayPal已经将其用于某些支付处理，要么建立自己的支付技术来鼓励人们继续购买该应用程序。 Instagram目前没有从交易中赚钱-今年已经免除了销售费用-但购物收入对于Instagram业务的增长将变得至关重要。不过，Instagram在商业上已经有了坚实的开端，如果没有Facebook的帮助，它可能会继续发展。 
当然，广告商和商店希望在Instagram上购买空间的原因是它能够通过对用户最有吸引力的产品和广告来定位用户。这些数据大部分是通过Facebook引入的，这对Facebook和Instagram而言都是重大损失，并且可能减少这两个平台对用户的了解。如果没有这种精确的定位，Instagram可能无法为广告收取高额费用，但它仍然拥有品牌想要吸引的庞大用户群。
  如果分拆出去，Instagram遭受的最大损失将是Facebook的适度投资。尽管存在种种失误，但从其空前的监督委员会到近年来雇用的一小撮国际主持人，Facebook在主持人的节制政策和执法方面投入了大量资金。尽管大部分失败归咎于Facebook，但Instagram却从中受益，Instagram能够实施预制政策并在较大平台上被标记后删除内容。分拆后，Instagram将不得不从头开始构建自己的系统，即使是最成功的平台也要完成艰巨的任务。
 但是尽管有很大的障碍，Facebook的所有权感觉更像是一种负债而不是资产。该平台是一个温和的混乱局面，引发了种族灭绝，影响了选举，并使世界两极化。尽管错误信息和阴谋论仍然在该平台上盛行，但Instagram奇迹般地避免了大多数这种反冲。它缺乏组织和推荐算法，不会在用户面前散布虚假新闻，这有助于它避免了Facebook最严重的问题。如果分开，Instagram可以完全摆脱Facebook的问题。
 Instagram已成为许多人的首选应用程序，有时会取代他们对Facebook的使用。在Facebook的指导下，该应用程序变得功能强大，具有比以往更多的格式。它不再只是照片应用程序。但是对于依赖Instagram推广业务或吸引新受众的用户，他们可能更喜欢Facebook与该应用程序完全无关。 Instagram可以独立存在，不受Facebook问题的困扰，并且可以再次成为独立的在线场所，使人们可以与他们关心的人和事物真正建立联系。
note_en:
For years, critics have argued that Facebook’s acquisition of Instagram was a missed opportunity to rein in the company. But now, they’ll have the chance to actually undo it. On Wednesday afternoon,  the Federal Trade Commission joined with 48 state attorneys general to call for a court-ordered divestiture of Instagram and WhatsApp — essentially, forcing Facebook to undo both acquisitions and spin them back into independent companies.
 Regulators are still a long way from making that happen, but for the first time, it’s a possibility that Facebook can’t ignore. And for Instagram users, it raises a question that’s both alarming and tantalizing: what would Instagram look like without Facebook?
 In the years since the 2012 acquisition, Instagram has benefited hugely from its relationship with Facebook, gaining massive scale and freeing itself from the pressure to achieve profitability. Even now, Facebook’s support is crucial to Instagram’s aggressive product development cycle and low-profile navigation of content moderation challenges. If regulators succeed, Instagram would have to build much of that from scratch.
 At the same time, there are reasons to think Instagram might be better off without Facebook’s baggage and might actually thrive outside the Menlo Park bubble. The Facebook name and tie mean an association with a chaotic mess of a platform and a branding nightmare. Every day Instagram stays within the Facebook family is another where it’s at risk for user backlash because of its parent company’s actions. Without that headache, Instagram could focus on what made it a popular app in the first place.
  Less than 24 hours after the cases were announced, Facebook has yet to mount its full defense against the antitrust lawsuits. But  in a blog post yesterday, the company’s general counsel gave an early preview of its case, arguing a spinoff would be both unfair and unfeasible. “No American antitrust enforcer has ever brought a case like this before, and for good reason,” wrote general counsel Jennifer Newstead. “The FTC and states stood by for years while Facebook invested billions of dollars and millions of hours to make Instagram and WhatsApp into the apps that users enjoy today.”
 But peeling apart a conglomerate isn’t quite as difficult as Newstead makes it sound. Companies do it all the time when they see profit in it, whether it’s Hewlett-Packard’s  spinoff of its enterprise business in 2017 or IAC’s spinoff of Match Group  earlier this year. Of course, it’s different when it’s mandated by the government, but even that has a fairly recent precedent in the Bell breakup of the ‘80s.
 After eight years of corporate integration, a spinoff of Instagram would surely be difficult. Facebook bought Instagram for $1 billion in 2012 during a quainter time for both companies, and since then, Facebook has attempted to tightly integrate the two apps. From the onset, it boosted Instagram’s visibility by tagging photos posted to Facebook from Instagram with a link to the app and prompted people to follow their Facebook friends on Instagram.
 “Instagram was easier to buy than to build because once a network takes off, there are few reasons to join a smaller one,” writes Sarah Frier in her book   No Filter: The Inside Story of Instagram. “It becomes part of the infrastructure of society.”
  Facebook also backed the company financially and shared its ad technology to help it actually make money, which Instagram wasn’t doing prior to the acquisition. But even though Facebook eventually ended its promotional support, its network effects already took hold: Instagram now has 1 billion users and ads all over the place. In the most important areas, Facebook’s work is already done. If Instagram spun off now, those crucial changes would remain in place.
 The remaining integrations with Facebook seemingly work more for Facebook’s own good than Instagram’s. In recent years, Facebook has tried even harder to link the two apps, allowing users to cross-post their content across both Facebook and Instagram. Facebook Messenger  is now available through Instagram, tying the platforms together in a single, unified messaging system. But for Instagram users, those integrations mostly serve to make Facebook that much more inescapable.
 It’s these tight ties that reportedly pushed Instagram co-founders Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger  to resign. Both executives quit after Facebook  reportedly revoked its growth support and tested new designs and features while Systrom was away on paternity leave, including a hamburger menu and location tracking.
 The Messenger integration is the most obvious place where users might be disadvantaged — people on Facebook wouldn’t be able to message a friend who’s on Instagram and vice versa — but that feature is fairly recent and only intermittently visible to users. Before now, users seemed to be fine with siloing off their respective platform friends, so it’s hard to argue it would be traumatic to put the silos back in place.
  The biggest question is whether Instagram could survive without Facebook’s money. Facebook has never broken out Instagram’s financials, so we have no official idea whether the platform is profitable on its own. However,  Bloomberg  wrote early this year that Instagram generated more than a quarter of Facebook’s revenue in 2019, or about $20 billion from advertising. That’s obviously significant and more than most social competitors, like Snap, bring in on their own. We don’t know Instagram’s independent operating costs, but with $20 billion in only advertising revenue, it’s easy to imagine the team could still manage to profit while developing new products.
 One area Instagram could struggle without Facebook is its burgeoning commerce business. This is an area where the Facebook infrastructure certainly helps Instagram and also where the company would have to invest. Right now,  Facebook Pay allows Instagram users to buy products directly from the platform. Facebook doesn’t license Facebook Pay out to third-party apps yet, so Instagram would have to either continue a relationship with PayPal, which it also already uses for some payment processing, or build its own payment technology to encourage people to keep buying from the app. Instagram currently isn’t making money off transactions —  selling fees have been waived for this year — but shopping revenue would become critical to growing Instagram’s business. Already, though, Instagram has a solid start on commerce that it could likely continue without Facebook’s help.
 Of course, the reason advertisers and shops look to buy space on Instagram is its ability to target users with products and ads that most appeal to them. Much of that data is brought in through Facebook, which would be a major loss for both Facebook and Instagram and could reduce how much both platforms know about their users. Instagram might not be able to charge as much for ads without that precise targeting, but it still has a massive user base that brands would want to reach.
  The biggest loss to Instagram if it spun off would be Facebook’s moderation investments. For all its blunders, Facebook has invested an enormous amount in moderation policy and enforcement, from its unprecedented  Oversight Board to the small army of international moderators hired in recent years. And while Facebook has taken the blame for most of the failures, Instagram has benefited from the response, able to implement premade policy and take down content after it’s been flagged on the larger platform. After a spinoff, Instagram would have to build its own system from scratch, a daunting task for even the most successful platforms.
 But despite that significant hurdle, Facebook ownership feels like more of a liability than an asset. The platform is a moderation mess that’s spurred  local genocide,  influenced elections, and  polarized the world. Instagram has, miraculously, avoided most of this blowback, although misinformation and conspiracy theories  still thrive on the platform. Its lack of groups and a recommendation algorithm that doesn’t thrust fake news stories in front of users, however, have helped it dodge the worst of Facebook’s problems. If separated, Instagram could rid itself of Facebook’s issues entirely.
 Instagram has become the go-to app for many people, sometimes replacing their use of Facebook. Under Facebook’s guidance, the app’s become more feature-packed with more formats than ever. It’s no longer just a photo app. But for the users who rely on Instagram to promote their business or reach new audiences, they might prefer if Facebook had nothing to do with the app at all. Instagram could exist on its own, untainted by Facebook’s problems, and become, again, an independent place online where people can truly connect with the people and things they care about.
